text.title: Cohort SQL Patterns and Implementation Rules
text.summary: Three warehouse-native SQL patterns (acquisition, behavioral, feature-adoption cohorts), implementation rules, and agent gotchas for cohort analysis.

rule.statement: Always use COUNT(DISTINCT user_id), never COUNT(*) in cohort queries.
rule.rationale: Duplicate events from retry logic, client-side buffering, or ETL replays inflate retention percentages. COUNT(DISTINCT) is the only safe aggregate.

rule.statement: Store cohort tables in long-format (cohort, day_offset, retention_pct) and pivot only in BI tools.
rule.rationale: Wide-pivot in SQL is inflexible — adding a new day_offset value requires a schema change. BI tools pivot interactively. Long-format also enables dbt tests on the retention_pct column.

rule.statement: Materialize cohort_size as a column on every output row.
rule.rationale: Downstream tools should never recompute cohort_size via subquery. Recomputing is fragile to filter changes and expensive at scale.

rule.statement: Pin the retention metric definition in the model description; a silent change from login to core_action invalidates all historical comparisons.
rule.rationale: Cohort comparisons are only valid when the retention metric is identical across cohorts. Metric drift is invisible until someone notices a suspicious trend jump.

rule.statement: Use LEFT JOIN events ON user_id, not INNER JOIN, to preserve cohort_size denominator.
rule.rationale: INNER JOIN silently drops users with no activity in the join window, making cohort_size appear smaller than actual and inflating retention percentages.

section.title: Acquisition Cohort Query
section.p: Groups users by signup week, tracks login activity, calculates D1/D7/D30 retention.

example.reference: Production dbt incremental model — use this for warehouse deployments
example.note: For quick prototyping, the basic pattern below works on any SQL warehouse. Replace DATEDIFF syntax per engine: BigQuery uses DATE_DIFF(end, start, DAY); Snowflake/Redshift use DATEDIFF(day, start, end).
example.code: WITH cohorts AS ( SELECT DATE_TRUNC('week', signup_date) AS cohort_week, user_id, signup_date FROM users ), user_activity AS ( SELECT c.cohort_week, c.user_id, DATEDIFF('day', c.signup_date, e.event_date) AS days_since_signup FROM cohorts c LEFT JOIN events e ON c.user_id = e.user_id WHERE e.event_type = 'login' AND e.event_date >= c.signup_date ), retention_by_day AS ( SELECT cohort_week, days_since_signup, COUNT(DISTINCT user_id) AS active_users FROM user_activity GROUP BY 1, 2 ), cohort_sizes AS ( SELECT cohort_week, COUNT(DISTINCT user_id) AS cohort_size FROM cohorts GROUP BY 1 ) SELECT r.cohort_week, r.days_since_signup, r.active_users, c.cohort_size, ROUND(100.0 * r.active_users / c.cohort_size, 1) AS retention_pct FROM retention_by_day r JOIN cohort_sizes c USING (cohort_week) WHERE r.days_since_signup IN (1, 7, 30) ORDER BY 1, 2;

section.title: Behavioral Cohort Query
section.p: Splits users by a behavioral milestone (onboarding completion) to compare retention impact.

example.code: WITH cohorts AS ( SELECT u.user_id, u.signup_date, CASE WHEN oc.user_id IS NOT NULL THEN 'Completed' ELSE 'Not Completed' END AS cohort_type FROM users u LEFT JOIN onboarding_completed oc ON u.user_id = oc.user_id WHERE u.signup_date >= CURRENT_DATE - INTERVAL '30 days' ) -- Join to activity and aggregate as in acquisition cohort pattern above, -- grouping by cohort_type instead of cohort_week.

section.title: Feature-Adoption Cohort Query
section.p: Compares retention of users who adopted a specific feature in their first 7 days vs those who did not.

example.code: WITH feature_users AS ( SELECT DISTINCT user_id FROM events WHERE event_type = 'feature_x_used' AND event_date <= signup_date + INTERVAL '7 days' ), cohorts AS ( SELECT u.user_id, u.signup_date, CASE WHEN fu.user_id IS NOT NULL THEN 'Used Feature X' ELSE 'Did Not Use' END AS cohort_type FROM users u LEFT JOIN feature_users fu ON u.user_id = fu.user_id WHERE u.signup_date >= CURRENT_DATE - INTERVAL '90 days' ) -- Aggregate as in behavioral cohort pattern above.

antipattern.description: Using DATEDIFF without specifying the warehouse; BigQuery and Snowflake/Redshift have opposite argument order.
antipattern.reason: Silent wrong results — the query runs but returns incorrect day offsets. Always specify the warehouse in agent prompts and validate output against a known cohort week.

antipattern.description: Using the schedule + while True refresh loop from notebook examples in production code.
antipattern.reason: A long-running Python process with sleep is not reliable for production. Use Airflow DAG, Cloud Scheduler, or dbt Cloud scheduled runs instead.

antipattern.description: Joining on user PII (email, name) instead of user_id in cohort queries.
antipattern.reason: PII joins bypass anonymization controls and create GDPR compliance risk. Always join on opaque user_id.

text.reference: dbt incremental model for production use
dbt schema tests for cohort model
